author: Origen of Alexandria
interpretation: Origen reads 'this rock' as Peter's confession of faith rather than Peter's person. Every believer who makes the same confession becomes a 'rock' on which Christ builds. The church is not founded on one man but on the truth Peter proclaimed — that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of the living God. The keys are given to all who share Peter's faith.
context: Origen's 'confession' reading became influential in the Eastern church and was later adopted by many Reformers. It represents one of the three major patristic interpretive options for this verse.

author: John Chrysostom
interpretation: Chrysostom identifies 'this rock' as Peter's confession — the theological content of what Peter declared. He emphasizes that the gates of hell will not prevail against the church, meaning that even death itself cannot defeat the community built on this truth. Chrysostom reads the promise as applying to the church universal, not to any individual bishop or see.
context: Chrysostom's reading was consistent with the Eastern church's resistance to claims of papal supremacy. The East generally acknowledged Peter's primacy of honor without conceding jurisdictional authority to Rome.

author: Augustine of Hippo
interpretation: Augustine famously revised his own interpretation. In his earlier works, he read 'this rock' as Peter himself. In his Retractationes, he acknowledged that the rock could equally refer to Christ, whom Peter confessed. Augustine left both readings as legitimate possibilities, noting that the reader should decide which is more probable. He did not resolve the question definitively.
context: Augustine's willingness to retract and revise his own earlier position is remarkable. Both Catholic and Protestant interpreters have claimed his authority — Catholics citing his earlier reading, Protestants citing his later correction.

author: Thomas Aquinas
interpretation: Aquinas reads 'this rock' as Peter himself, who represents the foundation of the visible church. Christ gives Peter a unique authority — the keys of binding and loosing — that establishes the Petrine office as the guarantor of doctrinal unity. The promise extends beyond Peter's lifetime to his successors, grounding the papal office in dominical institution.
context: Aquinas's reading became the standard Catholic interpretation and was affirmed at the First Vatican Council (1870) in the definition of papal infallibility. It remains the official Catholic position.

author: Martin Luther
interpretation: Luther rejects the papal reading entirely. The rock is Peter's confession — the faith that Jesus is the Christ — not Peter's person or office. Luther argues that the keys were given to the entire church, not to one man, and that no passage of Scripture establishes one bishop's authority over all others. The papacy is a human institution, not a divine one.
context: Luther's anti-papal reading of Matthew 16:18 was one of the earliest and most consequential exegetical battles of the Reformation. It struck directly at the scriptural foundation claimed for papal authority.

author: John Calvin
interpretation: Calvin argues that Christ himself is the rock (following 1 Corinthians 3:11) and that Peter is a stone in the building, not the foundation. The word play between Petros and petra is a play on Peter's new role, not an equation of Peter with the foundation. Calvin further argues that even if Peter were the rock, this would not establish succession — an office given to a person does not automatically transfer to a successor.
context: Calvin's distinction between the apostolic person and the institutional office became a cornerstone of Reformed ecclesiology and its rejection of both papal and episcopal succession claims.

author: Oscar Cullmann
interpretation: Cullmann, a Protestant, argues that the rock is indeed Peter personally — not his confession or Christ. However, he insists this is a unique, unrepeatable commission to the historical Peter, not a transferable office. Peter was the foundation stone in a once-for-all sense, just as a foundation is laid once and then built upon. There is no succession because foundations are not replaced.
context: Cullmann's concession that Peter is the rock while denying succession was a landmark in ecumenical scholarship. It acknowledged the Catholic exegetical point while reaching a Protestant ecclesiological conclusion.

author: N.T. Wright
interpretation: Wright reads the passage in its Jewish context: Jesus is reconstituting Israel around himself, and Peter's confession identifies Jesus as the Messiah who will build a new temple-community. The 'rock' language echoes Abraham (the rock from which Israel was hewn in Isaiah 51:1–2), making Peter the Abraham of the renewed people of God. The gates of Hades refer to death's inability to hold this community's Lord.
context: Wright's Jewish-contextual reading avoids the Catholic-Protestant impasse by reframing the question entirely. The point is not church governance but Israel's reconstitution around the messianic confession.
